serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw Napa cabbage is approximately 76g (1 cup shredded), commonly used in salads, slaws, or as a fresh side vegetable.

nutrient_source.title: Napa cabbage (Wongbok), raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Napa cabbage (Wongbok), raw (cr_id F14010028). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Chinese cabbage (pe-tsai), raw (FDC ID 169975) for secondary validation of macro and micronutrient plausibility. HPB values used as primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — Chinese cabbage / leafy brassica analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw Napa cabbage (Wongbok) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on the published GI range for raw leafy brassica vegetables (cabbage family), which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 10–40) per Atkinson et al. 2021 and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 0.9 × 0.76) / 100 ≈ 0.22, rounded to 0.3 for a 76g serving. The extremely low carbohydrate content (0.9g/100g) makes the GL negligible regardless of GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6mg/100g) and high potassium (250mg/100g) — favourable Na:K ratio supporting blood pressure reduction
hypertension.guidance: Raw Napa cabbage is an excellent choice for hypertensive patients due to its extremely low sodium content (6mg/100g) and high potassium (250mg/100g), which together support the DASH dietary pattern and active blood pressure reduction; patients should avoid adding high-sodium dressings or sauces, and note that fermented preparations such as kimchi carry substantially higher sodium loads that must be assessed separately.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.9g/100g), low GI (~32), negligible glycaemic load, and 1.1g dietary fibre per 100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw Napa cabbage is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, providing only 0.9g carbohydrate and 1.1g dietary fibre per 100g with a low estimated GI of approximately 32, resulting in a negligible glycaemic load that has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose; patients should avoid pairing with high-sugar dressings or sauces, and should assess fermented kimchi preparations separately for added sugar and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium, high potassium, and cruciferous phytonutrients including sulforaphane precursors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw Napa cabbage is cardioprotective, containing zero fat, zero saturated fat, and zero cholesterol per 100g, with a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio and cruciferous sulforaphane precursors that have evidence-based anti-inflammatory and endothelial-protective properties; it is freely recommended as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern and may be consumed liberally in its raw form.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cruciferous vegetable with vitamin C (~27mg/100g) and sulforaphane precursors with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Napa cabbage is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its vitamin C content (~27mg/100g) and sulforaphane precursors provide anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to RA, and raw consumption maximises these bioactive compounds, while fermented kimchi preparations additionally offer probiotic benefit for gut-immune modulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Napa cabbage (Wongbok), also known as Chinese cabbage or pe-tsai, is a variety of Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis characterised by pale green, tightly packed oblong leaves with broad white midribs and crisp, tender texture. In its raw state it has a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a refreshing crunch, making it suitable for fresh salads, slaws, wraps, and crudités. It is a staple ingredient across East and Southeast Asian cuisines, most famously used as the primary vegetable in Korean kimchi, Chinese hot pots, Japanese nabe, and Singaporean and Malaysian stir-fries and soups. Nutritionally, raw Napa cabbage is extremely low in calories (11 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (0.9g/100g), with 1.1g protein, negligible fat, 1.1g dietary fibre, and only 6mg sodium per 100g. Its potassium content of 250mg/100g and very low sodium create an ideal DASH-diet profile for hypertension management. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 32 (low), with a negligible glycaemic load, making it safe for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. As a cruciferous vegetable, it contains sulforaphane precursors (glucosinolates) and vitamin C (~27mg/100g), both of which have documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, with zero cholesterol and zero saturated fat. Raw consumption preserves maximum sulforaphane and vitamin C bioavailability.

How many calories in raw Chinese cabbage?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14010028, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. Values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central (Chinese cabbage, pe-tsai, raw, FDC ID 169975) and are consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for leafy brassica vegetables, clearly documented as such.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for raw Napa cabbage — this is a naturally fat-free vegetable with no added fats in its raw state. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero consistent with the naturally zero fat content of this raw leafy vegetable. Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are set to 0.0 as these nutrients are not present in plant-based raw vegetables. Fields set to -1 (data not available in primary source): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — these detailed micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this food item; USDA FoodData Central (FDC 169975) reports approximate values for some of these (e.g. folate ~79mcg DFE, vitamin A ~16mcg RAE, water ~95.3g) which may be used for supplementary reference but are not incorporated as primary values to maintain source integrity. Vitamin C of 27mg/100g is referenced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC 169975) as an indicative value consistent with cruciferous vegetable literature; this is noted as a secondary cross-reference value. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate for raw leafy brassica vegetables; no direct GI measurement for Wongbok/Napa cabbage is available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input is retained as the carbohydrate content of 0.9g/100g is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds.
